cases.title: Teen Athlete Collapse During Practice
cases.presentation: A 14-year-old male (55 kg) collapses during football practice. Witnesses report he suddenly fell without warning. BLS providers arrive within 3 minutes and find him unresponsive with no pulse. The AED is applied and shows a rapid, disorganized, coarse oscillatory waveform with no identifiable QRS complexes.
cases.question: The AED shows coarse VF. What is the most appropriate first defibrillation dose for this patient?
cases.explanation: The first defibrillation shock for pediatric VF/pVT is 2 J/kg. For a 55 kg adolescent, this equals 110 J with a biphasic defibrillator. This is unsynchronized defibrillation, not synchronized cardioversion (the patient has no pulse).
cases.clinicalPearl: Adolescent athletes who collapse suddenly should prompt consideration of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy or long-QT syndrome as the etiology of VF.

cases.title: Refractory VF in a Toddler After Drowning
cases.presentation: A 2-year-old (12 kg) was pulled from a swimming pool after an estimated 5 minutes of submersion. After 4 minutes of bystander CPR, EMS arrives and initiates advanced resuscitation. The initial rhythm is VF. The team delivers a first shock at 2 J/kg and resumes CPR for 2 minutes. Rhythm recheck shows persistent VF. A second shock is delivered at 4 J/kg. After 2 more minutes of CPR, the rhythm remains VF and IV/IO access is now established.
cases.question: VF persists after two defibrillation attempts. What antiarrhythmic drug and dose should be administered next?
cases.explanation: After at least one unsuccessful shock for refractory VF/pVT, amiodarone 5 mg/kg IV/IO is the first-line antiarrhythmic. For a 12 kg child: 5 × 12 = 60 mg. Epinephrine may also be given but amiodarone specifically suppresses recurrent VF. Lidocaine is an alternative if amiodarone is unavailable.
cases.clinicalPearl: Drowning-related VF can be refractory to defibrillation due to profound hypothermia and acidosis — aggressively address reversible causes while continuing defibrillation attempts.

cases.title: Unstable Wide-Complex Tachycardia in a School-Age Child
cases.presentation: A 9-year-old (28 kg) is brought to the ED by parents after complaining of chest pain and dizziness during a school assembly. On arrival, the child is pale, diaphoretic, and confused. The cardiac monitor shows a regular, wide-complex tachycardia at 220 bpm. A pulse is present but weak and thready.
cases.question: This child has a hemodynamically unstable wide-complex tachycardia. What is the recommended first intervention?
options: D: Observation and IV fluids only
cases.explanation: A hemodynamically unstable tachycardia with a pulse warrants synchronized cardioversion at 0.5–1 J/kg. The patient has signs of poor perfusion (hypotension, altered mental status, poor pulses), making this an emergency. Unsynchronized defibrillation is for pulseless rhythms; adenosine is for stable SVT.
cases.clinicalPearl: Never delay cardioversion in an unstable patient to establish IV access or obtain a full workup — sedation is ideal but should not delay the procedure when the patient is in extremis.
